This is due to the fact that the line
```
tower_decodings, tower_labels, tower_gradients, tower_loss, accuracy =
    get_tower_results(n_steps, data_sets.train, optimizer)
```

```
train_tower_decodings, train_tower_labels = decode_batch(data_sets.train)
```

`Issuer.user` represents a specific Django user.

In Django, all authentication backends are required to take some sort of credential and return a matching user. The best way to do this in the context of ASAP is to validate the token, extract the issuer, and then look up the `iss` value and determine if it matches a user. If Django finds a user linked to that `iss` (via `Issuer`), the user is authenticated. If Django does not find a user, authentication fails even though the token is valid (since the service doesn't recognize the issuer)

This parameter/identifier's source type is `data`, which means it comes from a data member on the associated parent resource. Data members are accessed by `path`, which is a JMESPath query. Once JMESPath supports querying attributes of objects then this line should change from a `getattr` call to a `jmespath.search` or something along those lines. For now it works in most cases since `path` is just the name of an attribute (e.g. `VpcId`), but the spec allows for any JMESPath (e.g. `Items[0].id`).

As far as I know, there's nothing that's actually in the spec about this specifically. 

- Literals can be automatically interned by all implementations.
- Strings can be interned manually.
- PyPy can intern numbers automatically (well, it [fakes that](http://doc.pypy.org/en/latest/cpython_differences.html#object-identity-of-primitive-values-is-and-id))
- CPython uses freelists for things like lists and tuples (and dicts?), so in theory it's possible to observe two such "different" objects having the same ID, *so long as you don't keep a reference to them*. Likewise, PyPy fake-interns tuples.

I think that "keeping a reference" thing is generally enough to be safe, though, so long as you avoid things that can be written literally like strings and numbers (typically one sees `object`). You can keep the `_MARKER` at global module scope or if you want to be extra sure you can cons one up for every function invocation:
```python
marker = object()
value = rev_index.get(docid, marker)
if value is marker:
...
```

why truncate the microseconds?

also, on the off chance that the timestamp is generated where microseconds=0, `isoformat` actually omits them entirely. In that case, your slice will actually lop off the seconds.

```python
>>> from datetime import datetime
>>> d = datetime.utcnow().replace(microsecond=0)
>>> d.isoformat()
'2018-02-08T17:34:53'
>>> d.isoformat()[:-3]
'2018-02-08T17:34'
```

SoS looks pretty cool! Integrating with it is certainly possible, though it might be a little bit too much for me (personally) to take on any time soon though.

Side note: Users can use magics to reach other languages `%%R`, `%%scala`, if they have them installed / available. Users can use the single line variant `%R` or the whole cell variant `%%R`.  There are interfaces to pass data between these, there just isn't a UI control to toggle languages like is shown in SoS. It goes something like this:

```
In [14]: Z = np.array([1,4,5,10]) # Python land

# Send Z over to an R session

In [15]: %R -i Z mean(Z)
Out[15]: array([ 5.])

# Bring an R result over to Python

In [16]: %R -o W W=Z*mean(Z)
Out[16]: array([  5.,  20.,  25.,  50.])

# 🎉

In [17]: W
Out[17]: array([  5.,  20.,  25.,  50.])
```

Granted I don't think that's the best way (or the only way) to pass data between processes. It's certainly possible though.

dir1/dir2/module1.py::TestClass1::test1 [param1=1]
dir1/dir2/module1.py::TestClass1::test1 [param1=2]
dir1/dir2/module1.py::TestClass1::test2

for: hier_dir =1, hier_module=1, hier_class=1, hier_param=0
we report: dir1 -> dir2 -> module1.py -> TestClass1 -> test1 [param1=1]
                                                                                 -> test1 [param1=2]
                                                                                 -> test2

for: hier_dir =0, hier_module=1, hier_class=1, hier_param=0
we report: dir1/dir2/module1.py -> TestClass1 -> test1 [param1=1]
                                                                        -> test1 [param1=2]
                                                                        -> test2

for: hier_dir =1, hier_module=0, hier_class=0, hier_param=0
we report: dir1 -> dir2 -> module1.py::TestClass1::test1 [param1=1]
                                    -> module1.py::TestClass1::test1 [param1=2]
                                    -> module1.py::TestClass1::test2


for: hier_dir =1, hier_module=0, hier_class=0, hier_param=1
we report: dir1 -> dir2 -> module1.py::TestClass1::test1 -> test1 [param1=1]
                                                                                      -> test1 [param1=2]
                                    -> module1.py::TestClass1::test2
